race.name: Etape Caledonia
race.tagline: Scotland's premier closed-road sportive. 80 miles through Highland Perthshire in the shadow of Schiehallion — the fairy hill where they first weighed the Earth.

vitals.prize_purse: None. Timed but non-competitive. Finisher medal.
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple feed stations with water, energy food, and mechanical support

climate.primary: Scottish Highlands spring — cool, wet, unpredictable
climate.description: May in Highland Perthshire means 45-60F with the genuine possibility of rain, hail, wind, and sunshine within the same hour. Scottish weather doesn't follow forecasts — it follows its own logic. Exposed sections over moorland and along lochs amplify wind chill. Hypothermia is a real risk on cold, wet days. The best days are stunning; the worst are survival exercises.
challenges: Rain and wind are probable, not possible — pack accordingly
Temperature range of 40-60F with wind chill dropping lower on exposed sections
Hypothermia risk if wet and cold on moorland stretches
Four-season weather in a single ride is standard Scottish operating procedure

terrain.primary: Paved Scottish Highland roads with sustained rolling climbs
terrain.surface: Paved B-roads and A-roads through Highland Perthshire. Roads are narrower than continental European sportive routes. Surface quality is generally good but varies. Closed to traffic on event day.
features: Schiehallion — the conical 'fairy hill' dominates the landscape (backdrop, not climbed directly)
Glen Lyon — Scotland's longest glen, with a sustained climb out
Tummel Valley — loch-side riding with rolling terrain
Several sustained climbs of 3-8% gradient, none exceeding HC category
Closed roads: the entire course is traffic-free on race day

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Scotland's biggest closed-road sportive through Highland Perthshire. The scenery and closed-road format are genuine assets. The climbing is honest but not Alpine. T3 by score (56).
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Adventure=4 for Highland Perthshire scenery — glens, lochs, and Schiehallion. Race_quality=4 for closed roads (premium feature in UK cycling). Climate=3 (Scottish weather is a legitimate wildcard). Value=4 (good entry fee for a closed-road event). cultural_impact=1.

logistics.explanation: Edinburgh is 90 minutes south. Glasgow is 2 hours. Both have international airports, but you're driving into the Highlands on A-roads. Pitlochry itself is a small Victorian resort town — charming but limited. Hotels and B&Bs fill months ahead. Public transport exists but isn't practical with a bike. It's not remote by Scottish standards, but it's not a city-center start either.

length.explanation: 80 miles with 5,900 feet of climbing is a solid day out. Not an ultra-distance epic, but enough to separate prepared riders from optimistic ones. The rolling terrain means you're never coasting for long. Most riders finish in 5-7 hours.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads, fully closed to traffic. The Scottish B-roads are narrower than continental sportive routes, but the surface is good and the gradients are steady. No technical demands — just pedal uphill and don't crash on wet descents.

elevation.explanation: ~5,900 feet of climbing across 80 miles is honest mid-range elevation. The climbs are sustained but moderate — 3-8% gradients, nothing exceeding 15 minutes at threshold. This isn't the Alps or even the Lake District's steepest roads. It's rolling Highland terrain with character, not savagery.

climate.explanation: Scottish weather in May is the X-factor. It can be 55F and sunny with views across Loch Tummel to Schiehallion — genuinely one of Britain's great vistas. Or it can be 42F with horizontal rain and a headwind across the moor. Both are equally probable. You must prepare for the worst and hope for the best. The weather IS the adventure.

altitude.explanation: The highest point on the course is roughly 1,500 feet above sea level. Scotland's Highlands are low mountains by international standards. Altitude is not a factor.

adventure.explanation: Highland Perthshire is spectacular. Schiehallion's conical peak — the 'fairy hill of Caledonians' where Nevil Maskelyne first measured Earth's density in 1774 — dominates the landscape. Glen Lyon is Scotland's longest and most beautiful glen. Loch Tummel and Loch Rannoch provide water-mirror backdrops. The combination of ancient geology, sparse population, and weather-driven light makes this one of Britain's most photogenic rides. You are riding through deep Scotland.

prestige.explanation: The Etape Caledonia is Scotland's biggest sportive and one of the UK's premier closed-road events. That carries weight in British cycling. Internationally? Few cyclists outside the UK have heard of it. It doesn't have the cachet of L'Etape du Tour or the Maratona. It's a big deal domestically, modest globally.

race_quality.explanation: Closed roads are rare in UK cycling and transform the experience. No cars, no pinch points, no danger from traffic. For 80 miles you own the road. The organization (IMG/Skyline Events) is professional — wave starts, timed, well-stocked feed zones, mechanical support, medical coverage. The closed-road format alone puts this in the top tier of UK sportive organization.

experience.explanation: On a good weather day, the Etape Caledonia is one of Britain's best sportive experiences — closed Highland roads through glen and loch scenery with Schiehallion watching over everything. On a bad weather day, it's a cold, wet survival march with limited visibility. The experience variance is massive. At its best: genuinely memorable. At its worst: character-building.

community.explanation: 5,000-6,000 riders create a genuine mass-participation atmosphere. The UK sportive community is enthusiastic and supportive. Pitlochry embraces the event. Feed stations have a convivial energy. It's not the cultural phenomenon of the Tour of Flanders sportive, but it's Scotland's cycling weekend.

field_depth.explanation: Timed but not competitive. No prizes, no elite wave, no racing license required. The field is self-selecting — UK cyclists who want a challenging closed-road day out. Some fast riders push for Strava times, but it's not a race. The field is enthusiastic amateurs, not competitive athletes.

value.explanation: £65-85 for a fully closed-road, 80-mile sportive through the Scottish Highlands is excellent value. Comparable UK sportives charge more for open roads. The closed-road premium alone justifies the entry fee. Feed stations are well-stocked. You get a lot of event for the money.

expenses.explanation: Scotland is moderately priced. Pitlochry B&Bs run £80-150/night. Driving from Edinburgh or Glasgow is affordable. If flying internationally, Edinburgh flights are reasonable from European hubs. Budget £400-800 for a UK-based rider, £800-1,500 for an international trip.

biased_opinion.summary: The Etape Caledonia is Scotland's cycling crown jewel — 80 closed-road miles through Highland Perthshire with Schiehallion as your constant companion. The closed-road format is rare in Britain and transforms the experience from 'sportive with traffic anxiety' to 'proper European-style riding.' The scenery is world-class: glens, lochs, ancient mountains, and the kind of light that only Scotland delivers. The climbing is honest without being savage. The one variable you can't control is weather — and Scottish weather in May is a dice roll that can deliver paradise or purgatory with equal probability.
strengths: Closed roads: 80 miles of traffic-free Highland riding — a premium feature in UK cycling
Highland Perthshire scenery: Schiehallion, Glen Lyon, and Loch Tummel provide genuine visual drama
Value: excellent entry fee for a closed-road event of this scale
5,000+ riders create mass-participation energy without overcrowding
weaknesses: Scottish weather is a genuine gamble — cold rain turns the experience from sublime to miserable
No international prestige: this is a UK event, not a global destination sportive
Pitlochry accommodation fills fast — the town is small and the event is large
Climbing is moderate: if you want Alpine severity, this isn't it
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Britain's best closed-road sportive through Scotland's most beautiful terrain. Pack a rain jacket. Then pack another one.

final_verdict.one_liner: 80 closed-road miles through Highland Perthshire. Scotland's premier sportive in the shadow of the fairy hill.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want Britain's best closed-road sportive experience. Yes if Highland scenery matters more than Alpine climbing. Skip if you can't handle the genuine possibility of cold rain for 6 hours.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want closed roads with bigger climbs: try the Etape du Tour. If you want UK cycling with more edge: try the Fred Whitton Challenge. If you want Scottish cycling with more terrain: try the Etape Royale.

logistics.airport: Edinburgh (EDI) — 90 minutes. Glasgow (GLA) — 2 hours. Both well-connected to European and some transatlantic routes.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Pitlochry has hotels, B&Bs, and guesthouses but capacity is limited for 5,000+ riders. Book 3-6 months ahead. Aberfeldy and Blair Atholl are nearby alternatives. Camping available at local sites.
logistics.food: Pitlochry has a decent selection of pubs, cafes, and restaurants for a Highland town. The post-ride food at the event village is included.
logistics.packet_pickup: Saturday at the event village in Pitlochry. Registration and bike number collection.
logistics.parking: Event parking in Pitlochry with shuttle to start. Book parking in advance — spaces are limited.

history.origin_story: Launched in 2007, the Etape Caledonia established itself as Scotland's premier mass-participation cycling event. The closed-road format — unusual in the UK where most sportives share roads with traffic — immediately set it apart. The route through Highland Perthshire, circling Schiehallion and threading through Glen Lyon, showcases some of Scotland's most dramatic landscape. It quickly grew to 5,000+ riders and became the anchor of the Scottish cycling calendar.
notable_moments: 2007: First edition draws thousands to Pitlochry for Scotland's biggest closed-road sportive
The event established the template for premium UK closed-road sportives
Route passes Schiehallion — where Nevil Maskelyne's 1774 experiment first measured Earth's density
Glen Lyon, Scotland's longest glen, became synonymous with the event
history.reputation: Scotland's cycling showpiece. The closed-road format and Highland scenery make it a bucket-list UK sportive.

course_description.character: Rolling Highland roads through glen and loch scenery. The route loops through Highland Perthshire with Schiehallion's conical peak as the visual anchor. Climbs are sustained but moderate — 3-8% gradients through wooded glens and across open moorland. Descents are fast on narrow Scottish roads. The character shifts between sheltered glen riding and exposed moorland sections where wind and weather hit hard. It's a course that rewards steady riding and punishes impatience.

suffering_zones.label: Pitlochry Start
suffering_zones.desc: Wave start through the Victorian resort town. The road climbs gently out of the Tummel Valley. Legs are fresh and the field is dense. The first hint of Highland scenery opens up as you leave town.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Gentle climb on A-road, closed to traffic

suffering_zones.label: Schiehallion Approach
suffering_zones.desc: The route swings around Loch Tummel with Schiehallion rising to the north. On a clear day, the conical peak reflected in the loch is one of Scotland's great vistas. On a wet day, you see nothing but grey. The climbing begins in earnest.

suffering_zones.label: Glen Lyon
suffering_zones.desc: Scotland's longest and most beautiful glen. The road follows the River Lyon through a narrow valley flanked by steep hillsides. The climb out of Glen Lyon is the course's most sustained effort — not steep, but relentless. This is where the day starts to bite.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sustained climb, 4-6% gradient, sheltered glen road

suffering_zones.label: Moorland Crossing
suffering_zones.desc: Open moorland between the glens. Exposed to whatever the Scottish sky decides to deliver. Wind, rain, or sunshine — there's no shelter. The accumulated climbing starts to tell in the legs. The route turns back toward Pitlochry.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Exposed moorland road, rolling terrain, wind exposure

course_description.signature_challenge: Glen Lyon — the sustained climb out of Scotland's longest glen on tired legs, with 40 miles already ridden and the knowledge that the exposed moorland crossing still awaits.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Scottish Highland roads, rolling with sustained climbs
guide_variables.race_weather: Cool (45-60F), unpredictable — rain, wind, and sun can occur in any combination
race_challenges: Scottish weather: genuine possibility of cold rain for the entire ride
Sustained climbing through Highland glens — moderate gradient but relentless
Wind exposure on moorland sections between glens
Pacing across 80 miles of rolling terrain with no extended flat sections

non_negotiables.why: Scottish May weather can deliver hours of cold rain. A quality rain jacket, full-finger gloves, arm warmers, and a vest are mandatory pack items. Train in the rain if possible — wet descents on narrow roads require confidence.

non_negotiables.requirement: Rolling-terrain endurance — 5+ hour training rides on hilly roads
non_negotiables.why: The route rarely flattens out. You're either climbing, descending, or rolling for 80 miles. This cumulative load is different from flat-road endurance. Train on rolling terrain to prepare your legs for constant gradient changes.
The temptation to surge on every rise burns matches you'll need for Glen Lyon and the moorland crossing. Settle into a rhythm early and ride to heart rate or power, not speed.
